title: Vanya and Triangles
description: Vanya got bored and he painted n distinct points on the plane. After that he connected all the points pairwise and saw that as a result many triangles were formed with vertices in the painted points. He asks you to count the number of the formed triangles with the non-zero area.
input_spec: The first line contains integer n (1 <= n <= 2000) -- the number of the points painted on the plane. Next n lines contain two integers each x_i, y_i (-100 <= x_i, y_i <= 100) -- the coordinates of the i-th point. It is guaranteed that no two given points coincide.
output_spec: In the first line print an integer -- the number of triangles with the non-zero area among the painted points.

routes.name: Complementary counting via collinear groups
routes.description: Count all C(n,3) triples, then subtract the degenerate (collinear) triples. Group collinear points by representing each line canonically, and for each line with k points subtract C(k,3).

observations.title: Non-zero area means non-collinear
observations.description: A triangle has non-zero area if and only if its three vertices are not collinear. This means we can count all triples and subtract those that are collinear.

hints.nudge: When does a triangle formed by three points have zero area?
hints.question: If you could easily count the 'bad' triples (zero area), how would you get the 'good' ones?
hints.formal: answer = C(n, 3) - (number of collinear triples). A triple is collinear iff the cross product of any two edge vectors is zero.

observations.title: Group points by the line they lie on
observations.description: To count collinear triples, determine which points share a common line. For each pair of points, compute a canonical representation of the line through them and use a map to group all pairs belonging to the same line.

hints.nudge: If you iterate over all pairs of points, can you identify which pairs define the same line?
hints.question: How would you represent a line through two points so that any two pairs on the same line produce the same key?
hints.formal: For each pair (i, j), compute the direction (dx, dy) normalized by gcd and sign, plus a canonical point on the line (or use the line equation ax + by + c = 0 normalized by gcd). Store in a hash map: line -> set of point indices.

observations.title: Canonical line representation requires care
observations.description: Two pairs of points lie on the same line if they share the same slope and intercept. Represent the line by its direction vector (dx, dy) divided by gcd with a consistent sign convention, plus a scalar that pins the line's position. This avoids floating-point issues.

hints.nudge: Using floating-point slope and intercept can cause precision issues. How can you represent a line using only integers?
hints.question: If you have direction (dx, dy), how do you normalize it so that parallel pairs on the same line get the same key? What additional value distinguishes parallel lines?
hints.formal: Normalize (dx, dy) by dividing by gcd(|dx|, |dy|) and ensuring the leading nonzero component is positive. Then the line can be keyed by (dx/g, dy/g, cross) where cross = x1 * dy/g - y1 * dx/g, a value constant for all points on the line.

observations.title: Subtract C(k, 3) for each line with k collinear points
observations.description: After grouping, each line that contains k points contributes C(k, 3) = k*(k-1)*(k-2)/6 degenerate triples. Sum these over all lines and subtract from C(n, 3) to get the answer.

hints.nudge: If a line passes through k points, how many zero-area triangles does that produce?
hints.question: How many ways can you choose 3 points from k collinear points? What is the combinatorial formula?
hints.formal: For each line L with |L| = k >= 3, subtract C(k, 3) = k*(k-1)*(k-2)/6. Total answer = n*(n-1)*(n-2)/6 - sum over all lines of C(|L|, 3). Overall O(n^2) time.

routes.name: Direct O(n^3) brute force with cross product
routes.description: Iterate over all triples of points and check whether each triple forms a non-degenerate triangle using the cross product test. Feasible within the 4-second time limit for n <= 2000.

observations.title: Cross product detects collinearity
observations.description: Three points A, B, C form a non-degenerate triangle if and only if the cross product (B - A) x (C - A) is nonzero. This single integer computation replaces any area or slope calculation.

hints.nudge: How can you check whether three points are collinear using a single arithmetic expression?
hints.question: For points A = (x1, y1), B = (x2, y2), C = (x3, y3), what does the sign of (x2 - x1)*(y3 - y1) - (y2 - y1)*(x3 - x1) tell you?
hints.formal: cross = (B.x - A.x) * (C.y - A.y) - (B.y - A.y) * (C.x - A.x). Triangle has nonzero area iff cross != 0. All values fit in 32-bit integers since coordinates are in [-100, 100].

observations.title: Triple enumeration with three nested loops
observations.description: Enumerate all C(n, 3) triples using three nested loops with i < j < k. For each triple, compute the cross product and increment the count if it is nonzero.

hints.nudge: What is the simplest way to visit every unordered triple of points exactly once?
hints.question: How many iterations do three nested loops with i < j < k produce for n = 2000? Is it feasible?
hints.formal: for i in 0..n: for j in i+1..n: for k in j+1..n: if cross(P[i], P[j], P[k]) != 0: count += 1. Total iterations = C(2000, 3) ~ 1.3 * 10^9.

observations.title: Feasibility under the time limit
observations.description: C(2000, 3) is about 1.33 * 10^9 iterations. Each iteration does a few multiplications and a comparison. With a 4-second time limit, this passes in C++ but may be tight. Minimize overhead inside the inner loop -- precompute coordinate differences or use simple integer arithmetic.

hints.nudge: With n = 2000, how many triples are there? Does the generous time limit help?
hints.question: What is C(2000, 3)? At roughly 10^8-10^9 simple operations per second in C++, is 1.33 * 10^9 iterations feasible in 4 seconds?
hints.formal: C(2000, 3) = 1,331,334,000. At ~3-5 * 10^8 cross-product checks per second in optimized C++, this takes 2-4 seconds. The 4s limit makes it borderline. In Python this approach will TLE; use the O(n^2) complementary counting route instead.
